plainMeaning: I bow to the Upadhyayas — the learned monks who teach the scriptures to other ascetics.

wordByWord.etymology: From Sanskrit namas (नमस्), 'bowing, homage', from the root nam- 'to bend, bow'; before a voiced sound namas → namo by sandhi.
wordByWord.notes: The same homage-particle opening each of the five bows; here it governs the dative-plural object.

wordByWord.gloss: to the upādhyāyas (the teaching-monks who instruct ascetics in scripture)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit uvajjhāya = Sanskrit upādhyāya, from upa- ('near') + adhyāya ('lesson, study', from adhi-√i 'to study'): 'one near whom one studies'. Prakrit softens upa- → uva- and assimilates -dhy- → -jjh-. The ending -āṇaṁ is the dative/genitive plural (Sanskrit -ānām).
wordByWord.notes: Single sandhi-compacted word carrying both the noun and its case-ending; 100% of the verse's content sits here plus 'namo'.

idiomaticTranslation: I bow in homage to the Upādhyāyas — the scripture-teaching monks.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This fourth bow descends one rung from the previous line's Ācārya to the Upādhyāya, the teaching-monk whose office is instruction rather than governance. Where the Ācārya heads the order and embodies conduct, the Upādhyāya is the custodian and transmitter of śruta (scriptural knowledge): he reads the Aṅgas and Upāṅgas with the younger ascetics and keeps the canon alive on the tongue. The same dative-plural gesture — namo + '-āṇaṁ' — again offers homage to an office and its qualities, not to any named person; tradition enumerates the Upādhyāya's excellence as mastery of the twenty-five scriptural sections he is charged to teach. The placement is deliberate and pedagogical: the verse moves the reciter from the one who leads (Ācārya) to the one who teaches (Upādhyāya), and the very next line will widen to 'all sādhus in the world' — so this line is the hinge between the singular guiding offices and the whole body of monks. To bow here is to honour learning itself as a rung of the path: the soul advances not only by example and discipline but by hearing and study, and the Upādhyāya is reverenced as the living conduit of that hearing.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The Upādhyāya occupies the fourth of the pañca-parameṣṭhi as the embodiment of right knowledge (samyag-jñāna) in its transmissive mode. Veneration falls on his function — guarding and teaching the Jina's śruta — rather than on liberation attained, distinguishing him from the Arihant and Siddha who are honoured for states reached. He is thus a 'guide along the way,' venerated alongside Ācārya and Sādhu as one who carries others forward.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this line, a practitioner honours every teacher who has handed down the scriptures, and resolves to value disciplined study — to learn the texts accurately and to pass them on faithfully — as itself an act of reverence.

crossReferences.text: Upādhyāya as fourth Parameṣṭhin, custodian of śruta
crossReferences.source: Tattvārtha-sūtra commentarial tradition; standard pañca-parameṣṭhi exposition

sources.title: Namokar Mantra — text, transliteration, history and the five Parameṣṭhins
sources.consultedFor: Placement of 'Namo Uvajjhayanam' as the fourth bow, the dative-plural -anam/-āṇaṁ ending, and the identity of the Upādhyāya among the pañca-parameṣṭhi.

sources.title: Upadhyaya (Jain teaching ascetic)
sources.consultedFor: Role of the Upādhyāya as scripture-teaching monk ranked below the Ācārya and his function of instructing ascetics.

sources.title: Monier-Williams / Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon — upādhyāya (upa + adhyāya)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology of upādhyāya from upa- + adhyāya (adhi-√i) and the Prakrit derivation uvajjhāya.

notes: Verse text and orthography follow the book verbatim, including 'ઉવજ્ઝાય' with the conjunct જ્ઝ. The closing period ('.') is the book's punctuation and is preserved. The grammatical ending '-āṇaṁ' is read as dative-plural ('reverence to…'), with genitive-plural an equivalent reading; both are standard for this formula. The traditional count of the Upādhyāya's twenty-five qualities (mastery of twenty-five scriptural sections) is the received understanding and varies slightly between Śvetāmbara and Digambara enumerations.
